revision.text:
{{Use mdy dates|date=December 2022}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ube Jinja
宇倍神社 | image = Ube-jinja, haiden-1.jpg | caption = Haiden of Ube Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Tottori Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|28|51|N|134|16|0.8|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Takenouchi no Sukune]] | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =651 Ichinomiya, Kokufu-cho, Tottori-shi, Tottori-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://www.ubejinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 21 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ube Jinja'''|宇倍神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Kokufu-cho neighborhood of the city of [[Tottori, Tottori|Tottori]] in [[Tottori Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Inaba Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 21.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ube Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]'''|武内宿禰|1=Takenouchi no Sukune|}}, the legendary Japanese hero-statesman of the 1st century, who is also regarded as a deity of longevity. ==History== The origins of Ube Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, it is believed that it began as the family shrine for the Ifubuki clan of [[Kofun period]], who were the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Inaba, and who possessed sacred swords given to them by [[:en:Emperor Seimu|Emperor Seimu]]. According to the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'', during the reign of the legendary [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]], Takenouchi no Sukune, who was over 360 years old at the time, went missing in Kamekinzan on the hillside of Mt. Ube in Inaba Province. There are two monoliths behind the shrine which are part of a ''[[kofun]]'' said to be Takenouchi no Sukune's [[Kofun]]. The shrine is located near the site of the [[kokufu|provincialcapital]] of Inaba, and there are many archaeological sites in the vicinity. In the [[Muromachi period]], the shrine gradually lost its estates and fell into decline. In 1581, when [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] attacked [[Tottori Castle]], the shrine was reduced to ashes. It was reconstructed in 1633 with the assistance of [[Ikeda Mitsunaka]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[Tottori Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The position of ''[[kannushi]]'' at the shrine has been a hereditary position of the Ifubuki clan since ancient times. The composer [[:en:Akira Ifukube|Akira Ifukube]] is the grandson of the 65th generation ''kannushi''. The shrine has been rebuilt frequently since it was founded, and the current main shrine was rebuilt in 1898. The [[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] of Ube Jinja is depicted on the 5-yen bank note in circulation from 1899 to 1934. The shrine is located a 20-minute walk from [[Tottori Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[San'in Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Ube-jinja, shaden.jpg|Precincts File:Ube-jinja, honden-1.jpg|Honden File:Ube-jinja, honden-2.jpg|Honden (back side） File:Ube-jinja, Kamegane-no-oka.jpg|Kamegane no oka Ube-jinja, Souriseki-2.jpg|Kofun alleged to be grave of Takenouchi no Sukune File:Ube-jinja, sekihi.jpg|Centotaph to Takenouchi no Sukune ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Ube Shrine}} *{{Official website|https://www.ubejinja.or.jp/}} {{In lang|ja}} *[https://www.tottori-guide.jp/tourism/tour/view/12 Tottori Tourist Information] *[https://www.city.tottori.lg.jp/www/contents/1185187917297/index.html Tottori City home page] {{Shinto shrine}} ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tottori (city)]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:11 6 3]] [[sn:Ube Shrine]]}} [[file:Series Ko 5 Yen Bank of Japan note - obverse.jpg|right|thumb|5-Yen banknote with Takenouchi no Sukune and Ube Jinja]] [[sn:Ube Shrine]] [[en:Ube Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ube Shrine}} [[fr:Ube-jinja]] [[ja:宇倍神社]] [[zh:宇倍神社]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]]

revision.text:
{{Use mdy dates|date=December 2022}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ube Jinja
宇倍神社 | image = Ube-jinja, haiden-1.jpg | caption = Haiden of Ube Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Tottori Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|28|51|N|134|16|0.8|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Takenouchi no Sukune]] | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =651 Ichinomiya, Kokufu-cho, Tottori-shi, Tottori-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://www.ubejinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 21 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ube Jinja'''|宇倍神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Kokufu-cho neighborhood of the city of [[Tottori, Tottori|Tottori]] in [[Tottori Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Inaba Province|Inaba Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 21.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ube Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]'''|武内宿禰|1=Takenouchi no Sukune|}}, the legendary Japanese hero-statesman of the 1st century, who is also regarded as a deity of longevity. ==History== The origins of Ube Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, it is believed that it began as the family shrine for the Ifubuki clan of [[Kofun period]], who were the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Inaba, and who possessed sacred swords given to them by [[:en:Emperor Seimu|Emperor Seimu]]. According to the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'', during the reign of the legendary [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]], Takenouchi no Sukune, who was over 360 years old at the time, went missing in Kamekinzan on the hillside of Mt. Ube in Inaba Province. There are two monoliths behind the shrine which are part of a ''[[kofun]]'' said to be Takenouchi no Sukune's [[Kofun]]. The shrine is located near the site of the [[kokufu|provincialcapital]] of Inaba, and there are many archaeological sites in the vicinity. In the [[Muromachi period]], the shrine gradually lost its estates and fell into decline. In 1581, when [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] attacked [[Tottori Castle]], the shrine was reduced to ashes. It was reconstructed in 1633 with the assistance of [[Ikeda Mitsunaka]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[Tottori Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The position of ''[[kannushi]]'' at the shrine has been a hereditary position of the Ifubuki clan since ancient times. The composer [[:en:Akira Ifukube|Akira Ifukube]] is the grandson of the 65th generation ''kannushi''. The shrine has been rebuilt frequently since it was founded, and the current main shrine was rebuilt in 1898. The [[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] of Ube Jinja is depicted on the 5-yen bank note in circulation from 1899 to 1934. The shrine is located a 20-minute walk from [[Tottori Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[San'in Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Ube-jinja, shaden.jpg|Precincts File:Ube-jinja, honden-1.jpg|Honden File:Ube-jinja, honden-2.jpg|Honden (back side） File:Ube-jinja, Kamegane-no-oka.jpg|Kamegane no oka Ube-jinja, Souriseki-2.jpg|Kofun alleged to be grave of Takenouchi no Sukune File:Ube-jinja, sekihi.jpg|Centotaph to Takenouchi no Sukune ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Ube Shrine}} *{{Official website|https://www.ubejinja.or.jp/}} {{In lang|ja}} *[https://www.tottori-guide.jp/tourism/tour/view/12 Tottori Tourist Information] *[https://www.city.tottori.lg.jp/www/contents/1185187917297/index.html Tottori City home page] {{Shinto shrine}} ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tottori (city)]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:11 6 3]] [[sn:Ube Shrine]]}} [[file:Series Ko 5 Yen Bank of Japan note - obverse.jpg|right|thumb|5-Yen banknote with Takenouchi no Sukune and Ube Jinja]] [[sn:Ube Shrine]] [[en:Ube Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ube Shrine}} [[fr:Ube-jinja]] [[ja:宇倍神社]] [[zh:宇倍神社]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]]

revision.text:
{{Use mdy dates|date=December 2022}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ube Jinja
宇倍神社 | image = Ube-jinja, haiden-1.jpg | caption = Haiden of Ube Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Tottori Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|28|51|N|134|16|0.8|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Takenouchi no Sukune]] | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =651 Ichinomiya, Kokufu-cho, Tottori-shi, Tottori-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://www.ubejinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 21 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ube Jinja'''|宇倍神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Kokufu-cho neighborhood of the city of [[Tottori, Tottori|Tottori]] in [[Tottori Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Inaba Province|Inaba Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 21.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ube Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]'''|武内宿禰|1=Takenouchi no Sukune|}}, the legendary Japanese hero-statesman of the 1st century, who is also regarded as a deity of longevity. ==History== The origins of Ube Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, it is believed that it began as the family shrine for the Ifubuki clan of [[Kofun period]], who were the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Inaba, and who possessed sacred swords given to them by [[:en:Emperor Seimu|Emperor Seimu]]. According to the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'', during the reign of the legendary [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]], Takenouchi no Sukune, who was over 360 years old at the time, went missing in Kamekinzan on the hillside of Mt. Ube in Inaba Province. There are two monoliths behind the shrine which are part of a ''[[kofun]]'' said to be Takenouchi no Sukune's [[Kofun]]. The shrine is located near the site of the [[kokufu|provincialcapital]] of Inaba, and there are many archaeological sites in the vicinity. In the [[Muromachi period]], the shrine gradually lost its estates and fell into decline. In 1581, when [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] attacked [[Tottori Castle]], the shrine was reduced to ashes. It was reconstructed in 1633 with the assistance of [[Ikeda Mitsunaka]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[Tottori Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The position of ''[[kannushi]]'' at the shrine has been a hereditary position of the Ifubuki clan since ancient times. The composer [[:en:Akira Ifukube|Akira Ifukube]] is the grandson of the 65th generation ''kannushi''. The shrine has been rebuilt frequently since it was founded, and the current main shrine was rebuilt in 1898. The [[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] of Ube Jinja is depicted on the 5-yen bank note in circulation from 1899 to 1934. The shrine is located a 20-minute walk from [[Tottori Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[San'in Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Ube-jinja, shaden.jpg|Precincts File:Ube-jinja, honden-1.jpg|Honden File:Ube-jinja, honden-2.jpg|Honden (back side） File:Ube-jinja, Kamegane-no-oka.jpg|Kamegane no oka Ube-jinja, Souriseki-2.jpg|Kofun alleged to be grave of Takenouchi no Sukune File:Ube-jinja, sekihi.jpg|Centotaph to Takenouchi no Sukune ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Ube Shrine}} *{{Official website|https://www.ubejinja.or.jp/}} {{In lang|ja}} *[https://www.tottori-guide.jp/tourism/tour/view/12 Tottori Tourist Information] *[https://www.city.tottori.lg.jp/www/contents/1185187917297/index.html Tottori City home page] {{Shinto shrine}} ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tottori (city)]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:11 6 3]] [[sn:Ube Shrine]]}} [[file:Series Ko 5 Yen Bank of Japan note - obverse.jpg|right|thumb|5-Yen banknote with Takenouchi no Sukune and Ube Jinja]] [[sn:Ube Shrine]] [[en:Ube Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ube Shrine}} [[fr:Ube-jinja]] [[ja:宇倍神社]] [[zh:宇倍神社]]

[http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Ube Shrine}} *{{Official website|https://www.ubejinja.or.jp/}} {{In lang|ja}} *[https://www.tottori-guide.jp/tourism/tour/view/12 Tottori Tourist Information] *[https://www.city.tottori.lg.jp/www/contents/1185187917297/index.html Tottori City home page] {{Shinto shrine}} ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tottori (city)]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:11 6 3]] [[sn:Ube Shrine]]}} [[file:Series Ko 5 Yen Bank of Japan note - obverse.jpg|right|thumb|5-Yen banknote with Takenouchi no Sukune and Ube Jinja]] [[sn:Ube Shrine]] [[en:Ube Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ube Shrine}} [[fr:Ube-jinja]] [[ja:宇倍神社]] [[zh:宇倍神社]]

revision.text:
{{Use mdy dates|date=December 2022}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ube Jinja
宇倍神社 | image = Ube-jinja, haiden-1.jpg | caption = Haiden of Ube Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Tottori Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|28|51|N|134|16|0.8|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Takenouchi no Sukune]] | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =651 Ichinomiya, Kokufu-cho, Tottori-shi, Tottori-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://www.ubejinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 21 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ube Jinja'''|宇倍神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Kokufu-cho neighborhood of the city of [[Tottori, Tottori|Tottori]] in [[Tottori Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Inaba Province|Inaba Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 21.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ube Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]'''|武内宿禰|1=Takenouchi no Sukune|}}, the legendary Japanese hero-statesman of the 1st century, who is also regarded as a deity of longevity. ==History== The origins of Ube Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, it is believed that it began as the family shrine for the Ifubuki clan of [[Kofun period]], who were the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Inaba, and who possessed sacred swords given to them by [[:en:Emperor Seimu|Emperor Seimu]]. According to the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'', during the reign of the legendary [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]], Takenouchi no Sukune, who was over 360 years old at the time, went missing in Kamekinzan on the hillside of Mt. Ube in Inaba Province. There are two monoliths behind the shrine which are part of a ''[[kofun]]'' said to be Takenouchi no Sukune's [[Kofun]]. The shrine is located near the site of the [[kokufu|provincialcapital]] of Inaba, and there are many archaeological sites in the vicinity. In the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the shrine gradually lost its estates and fell into decline. In 1581, when [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] attacked [[Tottori Castle]], the shrine was reduced to ashes. It was reconstructed in 1633 with the assistance of [[Ikeda Mitsunaka]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[Tottori Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The position of ''[[kannushi]]'' at the shrine has been a hereditary position of the Ifubuki clan since ancient times. The composer [[:en:Akira Ifukube|Akira Ifukube]] is the grandson of the 65th generation ''kannushi''. The shrine has been rebuilt frequently since it was founded, and the current main shrine was rebuilt in 1898. The [[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] of Ube Jinja is depicted on the 5-yen bank note in circulation from 1899 to 1934. The shrine is located a 20-minute walk from [[Tottori Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:San'in Main Line|San'in Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Ube-jinja, shaden.jpg|Precincts File:Ube-jinja, honden-1.jpg|Honden File:Ube-jinja, honden-2.jpg|Honden (back side） File:Ube-jinja, Kamegane-no-oka.jpg|Kamegane no oka Ube-jinja, Souriseki-2.jpg|Kofun alleged to be grave of Takenouchi no Sukune File:Ube-jinja, sekihi.jpg|Centotaph to Takenouchi no Sukune ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Ube Shrine}} *{{Official website|https://www.ubejinja.or.jp/}} {{In lang|ja}} *[https://www.tottori-guide.jp/tourism/tour/view/12 Tottori Tourist Information] *[https://www.city.tottori.lg.jp/www/contents/1185187917297/index.html Tottori City home page] {{Shinto shrine}} ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tottori (city)]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[sn:Ube Shrine]]}} [[file:Series Ko 5 Yen Bank of Japan note - obverse.jpg|right|thumb|5-Yen banknote with Takenouchi no Sukune and Ube Jinja]] [[sn:Ube Shrine]] [[en:Ube Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ube Shrine}} [[fr:Ube-jinja]] [[ja:宇倍神社]] [[zh:宇倍神社]]

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Use mdy dates|date=December 2022}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ube Jinja
宇倍神社 | image = Ube-jinja, haiden-1.jpg | caption = Haiden of Ube Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Tottori Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|28|51|N|134|16|0.8|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Takenouchi no Sukune]] | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =651 Ichinomiya, Kokufu-cho, Tottori-shi, Tottori-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://www.ubejinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 21 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ube Jinja'''|宇倍神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the Kokufu-cho neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Tottori, Tottori|Tottori]] in [[Tottori Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Inaba Province|Inaba Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 21.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ube Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]'''|武内宿禰|1=Takenouchi no Sukune|}}, the legendary Japanese hero-statesman of the 1st century, who is also regarded as a deity of longevity. ==History== The origins of Ube Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, it is believed that it began as the family shrine for the Ifubuki clan of [[Kofun period]], who were the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Inaba, and who possessed sacred swords given to them by [[:en:Emperor Seimu|Emperor Seimu]]. According to the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'', during the reign of the legendary [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]], Takenouchi no Sukune, who was over 360 years old at the time, went missing in Kamekinzan on the hillside of Mt. Ube in Inaba Province. There are two monoliths behind the shrine which are part of a ''[[kofun]]'' said to be Takenouchi no Sukune's [[Kofun]]. The shrine is located near the site of the [[kokufu|provincialcapital]] of Inaba, and there are many archaeological sites in the vicinity. In the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the shrine gradually lost its estates and fell into decline. In 1581, when [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] attacked [[:en:Tottori Castle|Tottori Castle]], the shrine was reduced to ashes. It was reconstructed in 1633 with the assistance of [[Ikeda Mitsunaka]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Tottori Domain|Tottori Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The position of ''[[kannushi]]'' at the shrine has been a hereditary position of the Ifubuki clan since ancient times. The composer [[:en:Akira Ifukube|Akira Ifukube]] is the grandson of the 65th generation ''kannushi''. The shrine has been rebuilt frequently since it was founded, and the current main shrine was rebuilt in 1898. The [[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] of Ube Jinja is depicted on the 5-yen bank note in circulation from 1899 to 1934. The shrine is located a 20-minute walk from [[Tottori Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:San'in Main Line|San'in Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Ube-jinja, shaden.jpg|Precincts File:Ube-jinja, honden-1.jpg|Honden File:Ube-jinja, honden-2.jpg|Honden (back side） File:Ube-jinja, Kamegane-no-oka.jpg|Kamegane no oka Ube-jinja, Souriseki-2.jpg|Kofun alleged to be grave of Takenouchi no Sukune File:Ube-jinja, sekihi.jpg|Centotaph to Takenouchi no Sukune ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Ube Shrine}} *{{Official website|https://www.ubejinja.or.jp/}} {{In lang|ja}} *[https://www.tottori-guide.jp/tourism/tour/view/12 Tottori Tourist Information] *[https://www.city.tottori.lg.jp/www/contents/1185187917297/index.html Tottori City home page] {{Shinto shrine}} ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tottori (city)]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[sn:Ube Shrine]]}} [[file:Series Ko 5 Yen Bank of Japan note - obverse.jpg|right|thumb|5-Yen banknote with Takenouchi no Sukune and Ube Jinja]] [[sn:Ube Shrine]] [[en:Ube Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ube Shrine}} [[fr:Ube-jinja]] [[ja:宇倍神社]] [[zh:宇倍神社]]

[http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Ube Shrine}} *{{Official website|https://www.ubejinja.or.jp/}} {{In lang|ja}} *[https://www.tottori-guide.jp/tourism/tour/view/12 Tottori Tourist Information] *[https://www.city.tottori.lg.jp/www/contents/1185187917297/index.html Tottori City home page] {{Shinto shrine}} ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tottori (city)]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[sn:Ube Shrine]]}} [[file:Series Ko 5 Yen Bank of Japan note - obverse.jpg|right|thumb|5-Yen banknote with Takenouchi no Sukune and Ube Jinja]] [[sn:Ube Shrine]] [[en:Ube Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ube Shrine}} [[fr:Ube-jinja]] [[ja:宇倍神社]] [[zh:宇倍神社]]

{{Use mdy dates|date=December 2022}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ube Jinja
宇倍神社 | image = Ube-jinja, haiden-1.jpg | caption = Haiden of Ube Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Tottori Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|28|51|N|134|16|0.8|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Takenouchi no Sukune]] | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =651 Ichinomiya, Kokufu-cho, Tottori-shi, Tottori-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://www.ubejinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 21 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ube Jinja'''|宇倍神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the Kokufu-cho neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Tottori, Tottori|Tottori]] in [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Inaba Province|Inaba Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 21.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ube Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]'''|武内宿禰|1=Takenouchi no Sukune|}}, the legendary Japanese hero-statesman of the 1st century, who is also regarded as a deity of longevity. ==History== The origins of Ube Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, it is believed that it began as the family shrine for the Ifubuki clan of [[Kofun period]], who were the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Inaba, and who possessed sacred swords given to them by [[:en:Emperor Seimu|Emperor Seimu]]. According to the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'', during the reign of the legendary [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]], Takenouchi no Sukune, who was over 360 years old at the time, went missing in Kamekinzan on the hillside of Mt. Ube in Inaba Province. There are two monoliths behind the shrine which are part of a ''[[kofun]]'' said to be Takenouchi no Sukune's [[Kofun]]. The shrine is located near the site of the [[kokufu|provincialcapital]] of Inaba, and there are many archaeological sites in the vicinity. In the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the shrine gradually lost its estates and fell into decline. In 1581, when [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] attacked [[:en:Tottori Castle|Tottori Castle]], the shrine was reduced to ashes. It was reconstructed in 1633 with the assistance of [[Ikeda Mitsunaka]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Tottori Domain|Tottori Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The position of ''[[kannushi]]'' at the shrine has been a hereditary position of the Ifubuki clan since ancient times. The composer [[:en:Akira Ifukube|Akira Ifukube]] is the grandson of the 65th generation ''kannushi''. The shrine has been rebuilt frequently since it was founded, and the current main shrine was rebuilt in 1898. The [[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] of Ube Jinja is depicted on the 5-yen bank note in circulation from 1899 to 1934. The shrine is located a 20-minute walk from [[:en:Tottori Station|Tottori Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:San'in Main Line|San'in Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Ube-jinja, shaden.jpg|Precincts File:Ube-jinja, honden-1.jpg|Honden File:Ube-jinja, honden-2.jpg|Honden (back side） File:Ube-jinja, Kamegane-no-oka.jpg|Kamegane no oka Ube-jinja, Souriseki-2.jpg|Kofun alleged to be grave of Takenouchi no Sukune File:Ube-jinja, sekihi.jpg|Centotaph to Takenouchi no Sukune ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Ube Shrine}} *{{Official website|https://www.ubejinja.or.jp/}} {{In lang|ja}} *[https://www.tottori-guide.jp/tourism/tour/view/12 Tottori Tourist Information] *[https://www.city.tottori.lg.jp/www/contents/1185187917297/index.html Tottori City home page] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tottori (city)]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[sn:Ube Shrine]]}} [[file:Series Ko 5 Yen Bank of Japan note - obverse.jpg|right|thumb|5-Yen banknote with Takenouchi no Sukune and Ube Jinja]] [[sn:Ube Shrine]] [[en:Ube Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ube Shrine}} [[fr:Ube-jinja]] [[ja:宇倍神社]] [[zh:宇倍神社]]

The shrine is located a 20-minute walk from [[:en:Tottori Station|Tottori Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:San'in Main Line|San'in Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Ube-jinja, shaden.jpg|Precincts File:Ube-jinja, honden-1.jpg|Honden File:Ube-jinja, honden-2.jpg|Honden (back side） File:Ube-jinja, Kamegane-no-oka.jpg|Kamegane no oka Ube-jinja, Souriseki-2.jpg|Kofun alleged to be grave of Takenouchi no Sukune File:Ube-jinja, sekihi.jpg|Centotaph to Takenouchi no Sukune ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q246447]] Wikidata name: Ube Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Use mdy dates|date=December 2022}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ube Jinja
宇倍神社 | image = File:Ube Shrine shaden.jpeg | caption = Haiden of Ube Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Tottori Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.4808|N|134.269|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Takenouchi no Sukune|qid=Q1073668}} | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =651 Ichinomiya, Kokufu-cho, Tottori-shi, Tottori-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://www.ubejinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 21 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Tottori|qid=Q200731}} }} {{nihongo|'''Ube Jinja'''|宇倍神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the Kokufu-cho neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Tottori, Tottori|Tottori]] in [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Inaba Province|Inaba Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 21.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ube Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]'''|武内宿禰|1=Takenouchi no Sukune|}}, the legendary Japanese hero-statesman of the 1st century, who is also regarded as a deity of longevity. ==History== The origins of Ube Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, it is believed that it began as the family shrine for the Ifubuki clan of [[Kofun period]], who were the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Inaba, and who possessed sacred swords given to them by [[:en:Emperor Seimu|Emperor Seimu]]. According to the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'', during the reign of the legendary [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]], Takenouchi no Sukune, who was over 360 years old at the time, went missing in Kamekinzan on the hillside of Mt. Ube in Inaba Province. There are two monoliths behind the shrine which are part of a ''[[kofun]]'' said to be Takenouchi no Sukune's [[Kofun]]. The shrine is located near the site of the [[kokufu|provincialcapital]] of Inaba, and there are many archaeological sites in the vicinity. In the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the shrine gradually lost its estates and fell into decline. In 1581, when [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] attacked [[:en:Tottori Castle|Tottori Castle]], the shrine was reduced to ashes. It was reconstructed in 1633 with the assistance of [[Ikeda Mitsunaka]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Tottori Domain|Tottori Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The position of ''[[kannushi]]'' at the shrine has been a hereditary position of the Ifubuki clan since ancient times. The composer [[:en:Akira Ifukube|Akira Ifukube]] is the grandson of the 65th generation ''kannushi''. The shrine has been rebuilt frequently since it was founded, and the current main shrine was rebuilt in 1898. The [[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] of Ube Jinja is depicted on the 5-yen bank note in circulation from 1899 to 1934. The shrine is located a 20-minute walk from [[:en:Tottori Station|Tottori Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:San'in Main Line|San'in Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Ube-jinja, shaden.jpg|Precincts File:Ube-jinja, honden-1.jpg|Honden File:Ube-jinja, honden-2.jpg|Honden (back side） File:Ube-jinja, Kamegane-no-oka.jpg|Kamegane no oka Ube-jinja, Souriseki-2.jpg|Kofun alleged to be grave of Takenouchi no Sukune File:Ube-jinja, sekihi.jpg|Centotaph to Takenouchi no Sukune ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Ube Shrine}} *{{Official website|https://www.ubejinja.or.jp/}} {{In lang|ja}} *[https://www.tottori-guide.jp/tourism/tour/view/12 Tottori Tourist Information] *[https://www.city.tottori.lg.jp/www/contents/1185187917297/index.html Tottori City home page] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tottori (city)]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[sn:Ube Shrine]]}} [[file:Series Ko 5 Yen Bank of Japan note - obverse.jpg|right|thumb|5-Yen banknote with Takenouchi no Sukune and Ube Jinja]] [[sn:Ube Shrine]] [[en:Ube Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ube Shrine}} [[fr:Ube-jinja]] [[ja:宇倍神社]] [[zh:宇倍神社]]

It was reconstructed in 1633 with the assistance of [[Ikeda Mitsunaka]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Tottori Domain|Tottori Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The position of ''[[kannushi]]'' at the shrine has been a hereditary position of the Ifubuki clan since ancient times. The composer [[:en:Akira Ifukube|Akira Ifukube]] is the grandson of the 65th generation ''kannushi''. The shrine has been rebuilt frequently since it was founded, and the current main shrine was rebuilt in 1898. The [[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] of Ube Jinja is depicted on the 5-yen bank note in circulation from 1899 to 1934. The shrine is located a 20-minute walk from [[:en:Tottori Station|Tottori Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:San'in Main Line|San'in Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Ube-jinja, shaden.jpg|Precincts File:Ube-jinja, honden-1.jpg|Honden File:Ube-jinja, honden-2.jpg|Honden (back side） File:Ube-jinja, Kamegane-no-oka.jpg|Kamegane no oka Ube-jinja, Souriseki-2.jpg|Kofun alleged to be grave of Takenouchi no Sukune File:Ube-jinja, sekihi.jpg|Centotaph to Takenouchi no Sukune ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q246447]] Wikidata name: Ube Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Use mdy dates|date=December 2022}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ube Jinja
宇倍神社 | image = File:Ube Shrine shaden.jpeg | caption = Haiden of Ube Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Tottori Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.4808|N|134.269|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Takenouchi no Sukune|qid=Q1073668}} | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =651 Ichinomiya, Kokufu-cho, Tottori-shi, Tottori-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://www.ubejinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 21 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Tottori|qid=Q200731}} }} {{nihongo|'''Ube Jinja'''|宇倍神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the Kokufu-cho neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Tottori, Tottori|Tottori]] in [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Inaba Province|Inaba Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 21.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ube Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]'''|武内宿禰|1=Takenouchi no Sukune|}}, the legendary Japanese hero-statesman of the 1st century, who is also regarded as a deity of longevity. ==History== The origins of Ube Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, it is believed that it began as the family shrine for the Ifubuki clan of [[Kofun period]], who were the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Inaba, and who possessed sacred swords given to them by [[:en:Emperor Seimu|Emperor Seimu]]. According to the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'', during the reign of the legendary [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]], Takenouchi no Sukune, who was over 360 years old at the time, went missing in Kamekinzan on the hillside of Mt. Ube in Inaba Province. There are two monoliths behind the shrine which are part of a ''[[kofun]]'' said to be Takenouchi no Sukune's [[Kofun]]. The shrine is located near the site of the [[kokufu|provincialcapital]] of Inaba, and there are many archaeological sites in the vicinity. In the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the shrine gradually lost its estates and fell into decline. In 1581, when [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] attacked [[:en:Tottori Castle|Tottori Castle]], the shrine was reduced to ashes. It was reconstructed in 1633 with the assistance of [[Ikeda Mitsunaka]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Tottori Domain|Tottori Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The position of ''[[kannushi]]'' at the shrine has been a hereditary position of the Ifubuki clan since ancient times. The composer [[:en:Akira Ifukube|Akira Ifukube]] is the grandson of the 65th generation ''kannushi''. The shrine has been rebuilt frequently since it was founded, and the current main shrine was rebuilt in 1898. The [[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] of Ube Jinja is depicted on the 5-yen bank note in circulation from 1899 to 1934. The shrine is located a 20-minute walk from [[:en:Tottori Station|Tottori Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:San'in Main Line|San'in Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Ube-jinja, shaden.jpg|Precincts File:Ube-jinja, honden-1.jpg|Honden File:Ube-jinja, honden-2.jpg|Honden (back side） File:Ube-jinja, Kamegane-no-oka.jpg|Kamegane no oka Ube-jinja, Souriseki-2.jpg|Kofun alleged to be grave of Takenouchi no Sukune File:Ube-jinja, sekihi.jpg|Centotaph to Takenouchi no Sukune ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Ube Shrine}} *{{Official website|https://www.ubejinja.or.jp/}} {{In lang|ja}} *[https://www.tottori-guide.jp/tourism/tour/view/12 Tottori Tourist Information] *[https://www.city.tottori.lg.jp/www/contents/1185187917297/index.html Tottori City home page] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tottori (city)]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] }} [[file:Series Ko 5 Yen Bank of Japan note - obverse.jpg|right|thumb|5-Yen banknote with Takenouchi no Sukune and Ube Jinja]] [[en:Ube Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ube Shrine}} [[fr:Ube-jinja]] [[ja:宇倍神社]] [[zh:宇倍神社]] {{wikidata link|Q246447}}

It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Inaba Province|Inaba Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 21.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ube Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]'''|武内宿禰|1=Takenouchi no Sukune|}}, the legendary Japanese hero-statesman of the 1st century, who is also regarded as a deity of longevity. ==History== The origins of Ube Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, it is believed that it began as the family shrine for the Ifubuki clan of [[Kofun period]], who were the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Inaba, and who possessed sacred swords given to them by [[:en:Emperor Seimu|Emperor Seimu]]. According to the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'', during the reign of the legendary [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]], Takenouchi no Sukune, who was over 360 years old at the time, went missing in Kamekinzan on the hillside of Mt. Ube in Inaba Province. There are two monoliths behind the shrine which are part of a ''[[kofun]]'' said to be Takenouchi no Sukune's [[Kofun]]. The shrine is located near the site of the [[kokufu|provincialcapital]] of Inaba, and there are many archaeological sites in the vicinity. In the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the shrine gradually lost its estates and fell into decline. In 1581, when [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] attacked [[:en:Tottori Castle|Tottori Castle]], the shrine was reduced to ashes. It was reconstructed in 1633 with the assistance of [[Ikeda Mitsunaka]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Tottori Domain|Tottori Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The position of ''[[kannushi]]'' at the shrine has been a hereditary position of the Ifubuki clan since ancient times. The composer [[:en:Akira Ifukube|Akira Ifukube]] is the grandson of the 65th generation ''kannushi''. The shrine has been rebuilt frequently since it was founded, and the current main shrine was rebuilt in 1898. The [[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] of Ube Jinja is depicted on the 5-yen bank note in circulation from 1899 to 1934. The shrine is located a 20-minute walk from [[:en:Tottori Station|Tottori Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:San'in Main Line|San'in Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Ube-jinja, shaden.jpg|Precincts File:Ube-jinja, honden-1.jpg|Honden File:Ube-jinja, honden-2.jpg|Honden (back side） File:Ube-jinja, Kamegane-no-oka.jpg|Kamegane no oka Ube-jinja, Souriseki-2.jpg|Kofun alleged to be grave of Takenouchi no Sukune File:Ube-jinja, sekihi.jpg|Centotaph to Takenouchi no Sukune ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Ube Shrine}} *{{Official website|https://www.ubejinja.or.jp/}} {{In lang|ja}} *[https://www.tottori-guide.jp/tourism/tour/view/12 Tottori Tourist Information] *[https://www.city.tottori.lg.jp/www/contents/1185187917297/index.html Tottori City home page] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tottori (city)]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] }} [[file:Series Ko 5 Yen Bank of Japan note - obverse.jpg|right|thumb|5-Yen banknote with Takenouchi no Sukune and Ube Jinja]] [[en:Ube Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ube Shrine}} [[fr:Ube-jinja]] [[ja:宇倍神社]] [[zh:宇倍神社]] {{wikidata link|Q246447}}

[http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Ube Shrine}} *{{Official website|https://www.ubejinja.or.jp/}} {{In lang|ja}} *[https://www.tottori-guide.jp/tourism/tour/view/12 Tottori Tourist Information] *[https://www.city.tottori.lg.jp/www/contents/1185187917297/index.html Tottori City home page] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tottori (city)]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] }} [[file:Series Ko 5 Yen Bank of Japan note - obverse.jpg|right|thumb|5-Yen banknote with Takenouchi no Sukune and Ube Jinja]] [[en:Ube Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ube Shrine}} [[fr:Ube-jinja]] [[ja:宇倍神社]] [[zh:宇倍神社]] {{wikidata link|Q246447}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

[[sn:commons:Category:Ube Shrine]][[en:Ube Shrine]][[fr:Ube-jinja]][[ja:宇倍神社]][[zh:宇倍神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q246447]] Wikidata name: Ube Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Use mdy dates|date=December 2022}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ube Jinja
宇倍神社 | image = File:Ube Shrine shaden.jpeg | caption = Haiden of Ube Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Tottori Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.4808|N|134.269|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Takenouchi no Sukune|WD=Q1073668}} | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =651 Ichinomiya, Kokufu-cho, Tottori-shi, Tottori-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://www.ubejinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 21 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Tottori|WD=Q200731}} }} {{nihongo|'''Ube Jinja'''|宇倍神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the Kokufu-cho neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Tottori, Tottori|Tottori]] in [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Inaba Province|Inaba Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 21.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ube Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]'''|武内宿禰|1=Takenouchi no Sukune|}}, the legendary Japanese hero-statesman of the 1st century, who is also regarded as a deity of longevity. ==History== The origins of Ube Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, it is believed that it began as the family shrine for the Ifubuki clan of [[Kofun period]], who were the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Inaba, and who possessed sacred swords given to them by [[:en:Emperor Seimu|Emperor Seimu]]. According to the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'', during the reign of the legendary [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]], Takenouchi no Sukune, who was over 360 years old at the time, went missing in Kamekinzan on the hillside of Mt. Ube in Inaba Province. There are two monoliths behind the shrine which are part of a ''[[kofun]]'' said to be Takenouchi no Sukune's [[Kofun]]. The shrine is located near the site of the [[kokufu|provincialcapital]] of Inaba, and there are many archaeological sites in the vicinity. In the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the shrine gradually lost its estates and fell into decline. In 1581, when [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] attacked [[:en:Tottori Castle|Tottori Castle]], the shrine was reduced to ashes. It was reconstructed in 1633 with the assistance of [[Ikeda Mitsunaka]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Tottori Domain|Tottori Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The position of ''[[kannushi]]'' at the shrine has been a hereditary position of the Ifubuki clan since ancient times. The composer [[:en:Akira Ifukube|Akira Ifukube]] is the grandson of the 65th generation ''kannushi''. The shrine has been rebuilt frequently since it was founded, and the current main shrine was rebuilt in 1898. The [[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] of Ube Jinja is depicted on the 5-yen bank note in circulation from 1899 to 1934. The shrine is located a 20-minute walk from [[:en:Tottori Station|Tottori Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:San'in Main Line|San'in Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Ube-jinja, shaden.jpg|Precincts File:Ube-jinja, honden-1.jpg|Honden File:Ube-jinja, honden-2.jpg|Honden (back side） File:Ube-jinja, Kamegane-no-oka.jpg|Kamegane no oka Ube-jinja, Souriseki-2.jpg|Kofun alleged to be grave of Takenouchi no Sukune File:Ube-jinja, sekihi.jpg|Centotaph to Takenouchi no Sukune ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Ube Shrine}} *{{Official website|https://www.ubejinja.or.jp/}} {{In lang|ja}} *[https://www.tottori-guide.jp/tourism/tour/view/12 Tottori Tourist Information] *[https://www.city.tottori.lg.jp/www/contents/1185187917297/index.html Tottori City home page] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tottori (city)]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] }} [[file:Series Ko 5 Yen Bank of Japan note - obverse.jpg|right|thumb|5-Yen banknote with Takenouchi no Sukune and Ube Jinja]] [[en:Ube Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ube Shrine}} [[fr:Ube-jinja]] [[ja:宇倍神社]] [[zh:宇倍神社]] {{wikidata link|Q246447}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

It was reconstructed in 1633 with the assistance of [[Ikeda Mitsunaka]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Tottori Domain|Tottori Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The position of ''[[kannushi]]'' at the shrine has been a hereditary position of the Ifubuki clan since ancient times. The composer [[:en:Akira Ifukube|Akira Ifukube]] is the grandson of the 65th generation ''kannushi''. The shrine has been rebuilt frequently since it was founded, and the current main shrine was rebuilt in 1898. The [[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] of Ube Jinja is depicted on the 5-yen bank note in circulation from 1899 to 1934. The shrine is located a 20-minute walk from [[:en:Tottori Station|Tottori Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:San'in Main Line|San'in Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Ube-jinja, shaden.jpg|Precincts File:Ube-jinja, honden-1.jpg|Honden File:Ube-jinja, honden-2.jpg|Honden (back side） File:Ube-jinja, Kamegane-no-oka.jpg|Kamegane no oka Ube-jinja, Souriseki-2.jpg|Kofun alleged to be grave of Takenouchi no Sukune File:Ube-jinja, sekihi.jpg|Centotaph to Takenouchi no Sukune ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Ube Shrine}} *{{Official website|https://www.ubejinja.or.jp/}} {{In lang|ja}} *[https://www.tottori-guide.jp/tourism/tour/view/12 Tottori Tourist Information] *[https://www.city.tottori.lg.jp/www/contents/1185187917297/index.html Tottori City home page] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tottori (city)]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] }} [[file:Series Ko 5 Yen Bank of Japan note - obverse.jpg|right|thumb|5-Yen banknote with Takenouchi no Sukune and Ube Jinja]] [[en:Ube Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ube Shrine}} [[fr:Ube-jinja]] [[ja:宇倍神社]] [[zh:宇倍神社]] {{wikidata link|Q246447}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] {{wikidata link|Q246447}} [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

[[sn:commons:Category:Ube Shrine]][[en:Ube Shrine]][[fr:Ube-jinja]][[ja:宇倍神社]][[zh:宇倍神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q246447]] Wikidata name: Ube Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Use mdy dates|date=December 2022}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ube Jinja
宇倍神社 | image = File:Ube Shrine shaden.jpeg | caption = Haiden of Ube Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Tottori Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.4808|N|134.269|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Takenouchi no Sukune|WD=Q1073668}} | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =651 Ichinomiya, Kokufu-cho, Tottori-shi, Tottori-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://www.ubejinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 21 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Tottori|WD=Q200731}} }} {{nihongo|'''Ube Jinja'''|宇倍神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the Kokufu-cho neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Tottori, Tottori|Tottori]] in [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Inaba Province|Inaba Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 21.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ube Jinja is: * {{nihongo|'''[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]'''|武内宿禰|1=Takenouchi no Sukune|}}, the legendary Japanese hero-statesman of the 1st century, who is also regarded as a deity of longevity. ==History== The origins of Ube Jinja are unknown. Although there is no documentary evidence, it is believed that it began as the family shrine for the Ifubuki clan of [[Kofun period]], who were the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Inaba, and who possessed sacred swords given to them by [[:en:Emperor Seimu|Emperor Seimu]]. According to the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'', during the reign of the legendary [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]], Takenouchi no Sukune, who was over 360 years old at the time, went missing in Kamekinzan on the hillside of Mt. Ube in Inaba Province. There are two monoliths behind the shrine which are part of a ''[[kofun]]'' said to be Takenouchi no Sukune's [[Kofun]]. The shrine is located near the site of the [[kokufu|provincialcapital]] of Inaba, and there are many archaeological sites in the vicinity. In the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the shrine gradually lost its estates and fell into decline. In 1581, when [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] attacked [[:en:Tottori Castle|Tottori Castle]], the shrine was reduced to ashes. It was reconstructed in 1633 with the assistance of [[Ikeda Mitsunaka]], the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Tottori Domain|Tottori Domain]] under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The position of ''[[kannushi]]'' at the shrine has been a hereditary position of the Ifubuki clan since ancient times. The composer [[:en:Akira Ifukube|Akira Ifukube]] is the grandson of the 65th generation ''kannushi''. The shrine has been rebuilt frequently since it was founded, and the current main shrine was rebuilt in 1898. The [[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] of Ube Jinja is depicted on the 5-yen bank note in circulation from 1899 to 1934. The shrine is located a 20-minute walk from [[:en:Tottori Station|Tottori Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:San'in Main Line|San'in Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Ube-jinja, shaden.jpg|Precincts File:Ube-jinja, honden-1.jpg|Honden File:Ube-jinja, honden-2.jpg|Honden (back side） File:Ube-jinja, Kamegane-no-oka.jpg|Kamegane no oka Ube-jinja, Souriseki-2.jpg|Kofun alleged to be grave of Takenouchi no Sukune File:Ube-jinja, sekihi.jpg|Centotaph to Takenouchi no Sukune ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Ube Shrine}} *{{Official website|https://www.ubejinja.or.jp/}} {{In lang|ja}} *[https://www.tottori-guide.jp/tourism/tour/view/12 Tottori Tourist Information] *[https://www.city.tottori.lg.jp/www/contents/1185187917297/index.html Tottori City home page] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tottori (city)]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] }} [[file:Series Ko 5 Yen Bank of Japan note - obverse.jpg|right|thumb|5-Yen banknote with Takenouchi no Sukune and Ube Jinja]] [[en:Ube Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ube Shrine}} [[fr:Ube-jinja]] [[ja:宇倍神社]] [[zh:宇倍神社]] {{wikidata link|Q246447}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]]{{wikidata link|Q1}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]]{{wikidata link|Q1}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]]{{wikidata link|Q1}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]]{{wikidata link|Q1}}[[Category:qq]] {{wikidata link|Q246447}} [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]]{{wikidata link|Q1}}[[Category:qq]]

[http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Ube Shrine}} *{{Official website|https://www.ubejinja.or.jp/}} {{In lang|ja}} *[https://www.tottori-guide.jp/tourism/tour/view/12 Tottori Tourist Information] *[https://www.city.tottori.lg.jp/www/contents/1185187917297/index.html Tottori City home page] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tottori (city)]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] }} [[file:Series Ko 5 Yen Bank of Japan note - obverse.jpg|right|thumb|5-Yen banknote with Takenouchi no Sukune and Ube Jinja]] [[en:Ube Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Ube Shrine}} [[fr:Ube-jinja]] [[ja:宇倍神社]] [[zh:宇倍神社]] {{wikidata link|Q246447}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] {{wikidata link|Q246447}} [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]]
